questions.question:
Give the correct matching for the following pairs:

(A) O(log n)    (P) Selection
(B) O(n)        (Q) Insertion sort
(C) O(n log n)  (R) Binary search
(D) O(n^2)      (S) Merge sort

A. A-R B-P C-Q D-S
B. A-P B-R C-S D-Q
C. A-P B-S C-R D-Q
D. A-R B-P C-S D-Q
questions.to_find: Match sorting algorithms / search techniques with their time complexities.

understand.plain: We are given four time complexities (O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2)) and four algorithms/operations (Selection, Insertion sort, Binary search, Merge sort). We must pair each complexity with its matching algorithm.

keywords.explain: A divide-and-conquer search on a sorted array that halves the search space each step.
keywords.example: Searching in an array of 1024 elements takes at most 10 comparisons.

keywords.explain: A sorting algorithm that preserves the relative order of equal elements.
keywords.example: Merge sort is stable; quick sort is generally not.

keywords.explain: A divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm with guaranteed O(n log n) time.
keywords.example: Splits array in half, sorts each half, then merges.

understand.visual_alt: Four algorithms mapped to four complexities: Binary search->O(log n), Selection->O(n) minimum comparisons for min element, Merge sort->O(n log n), Insertion sort->O(n^2) worst case.

given.aim: Match each algorithm/operation with its time complexity.

terms.example: Binary search on a sorted array
terms.connects: Binary search (R) halves the problem each step => O(log n)

terms.example: Finding minimum in an unsorted array
terms.connects: Selection (P) of minimum element needs O(n) comparisons
Merge sort (S) has O(n log n) time in all cases

terms.example: Bubble sort, insertion sort worst case
terms.connects: Insertion sort (Q) has O(n^2) worst-case time

given.plan: Recall the standard time complexities for each algorithm: Binary search is O(log n), selecting the minimum element takes O(n), merge sort is O(n log n), and insertion sort is O(n^2) in the worst case.

steps.title: Identify each algorithm's complexity
steps.apply:
Binary search (R): O(log n) => matches (A).
Selection (P) of minimum: O(n) => matches (B).
Merge sort (S): O(n log n) => matches (C).
Insertion sort (Q): O(n^2) worst case => matches (D).
steps.note: Selection here means finding the minimum (or k-th element) which is O(n), not selection sort which is O(n^2).

steps.title: Form the matching pairs
steps.apply:
A-R (O(log n) - Binary search), B-P (O(n) - Selection), C-S (O(n log n) - Merge sort), D-Q (O(n^2) - Insertion sort).
This corresponds to option D: A-R B-P C-S D-Q.
steps.note: Check all answer options: option D is A-R B-P C-S D-Q, which matches our analysis.

questions.formula_note: Standard time complexity results: binary search O(log n), linear scan/selection O(n), merge sort O(n log n), insertion sort O(n^2) worst case.

questions.question:
A sorting technique is called stable if

A. it takes O(n log n) time
B. it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements
C. it uses divide and conquer paradigm
D. it takes O(n) space
questions.answer: B. it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements
questions.to_find: The definition of a stable sorting algorithm.

understand.plain: This question asks what the property 'stable' means for a sorting algorithm. It is a definition-based question. Among the four options, only one correctly defines stability in sorting.

keywords.explain: A sorting algorithm is stable if, for any two elements with equal keys, the one that appeared first in the input still appears first in the output.
keywords.example: If array has (5, 'a') and (5, 'b') in that order, a stable sort keeps (5,'a') before (5,'b') in the output.

keywords.explain: Elements that compare as equal (same key value) but may differ in secondary attributes.
keywords.example: Two records with the same age are non-distinct with respect to age.

understand.visual_alt: Two equal-key elements A and B (A before B in input). Stable sort: A before B in output. Unstable sort: B may appear before A.

given.aim: Identify the correct definition of a stable sorting technique.

terms.meaning: Property of preserving relative order of equal elements
terms.example: Merge sort is stable; heap sort is not
terms.connects: Directly defines what 'stable' means in sorting context

terms.meaning: The order in which equal-key elements appear in the original input
terms.example: If red card and blue card both have value 5, relative order means red stays before blue
terms.connects: The key aspect of stability — equal elements keep their original ordering

terms.meaning: Elements that have the same key/sort value
terms.example: In array [3, 1, 3, 2], the two 3s are non-distinct
terms.connects: Stability only matters when there are non-distinct (equal) elements

given.plan: Recall the textbook definition of stability in sorting: it is about preserving relative order of equal elements, not about time complexity, paradigm, or space usage.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. O(n log n) time — This is about efficiency, not stability. Merge sort is O(n log n) and stable, but heap sort is also O(n log n) and NOT stable.
B. Maintains relative order of non-distinct elements — This is the textbook definition of stability.
C. Uses divide and conquer — Not related to stability. Both stable and unstable sorts can use divide-and-conquer.
D. Takes O(n) space — Space complexity is unrelated to stability.
steps.note: Many students confuse stability with efficiency or space. Stability is purely about behavior on equal elements.

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: A sorting algorithm is stable if and only if it preserves the relative order of equal-key elements from input to output. This matches option B exactly.
steps.note: Examples of stable sorts: Merge sort, Insertion sort, Bubble sort, Counting sort. Examples of unstable sorts: Quick sort, Heap sort, Selection sort.

solution.result: B. it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements

questions.formula_note: Definition of stable sorting: relative order of equal-key elements is preserved from input to output.

questions.question:
Let A be an n x n matrix such that the elements in each row and each column are arranged in ascending order. Draw a decision tree, which finds 1st, 2nd and 3rd smallest elements in minimum number of comparisons.

(Answer: state the minimum number of comparisons required to find the 3 smallest elements.)
questions.to_find: Minimum number of comparisons to find the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd smallest elements in a sorted row/column matrix.

understand.plain: We have an n x n matrix where every row is sorted in ascending order and every column is sorted in ascending order (like a Young tableau). We need to find the 3 smallest elements using as few comparisons as possible. The smallest is always at position (1,1) — that requires 0 comparisons. For 2nd and 3rd smallest we must compare candidates.

keywords.explain: An n x n matrix where rows and columns are both sorted in ascending order.
A binary tree where each internal node is a comparison and each leaf is an outcome/answer.
keywords.example: A decision tree for finding minimum of 3 elements has 2 internal nodes (2 comparisons).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220' viewBox='0 0 420 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='10' y='10' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='90' y='10' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='170' y='10' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a11</text><text x='120' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a12</text><text x='200' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a13</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='90' y='70' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a21</text><text x='120' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a22</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a31</text><text x='250' y='50' class='sub mut'>Candidates for 2nd: a12, a21</text><text x='250' y='75' class='sub mut'>Candidates for 3rd depend</text><text x='250' y='95' class='sub mut'>on 2nd smallest found</text><text x='250' y='130' class='tag ink'>1st = a11 (no comparisons)</text><text x='250' y='155' class='tag ink'>2nd: 1 comparison (a12 vs a21)</text><text x='250' y='180' class='tag ink'>3rd: 2-3 more comparisons</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: n x n matrix with rows and columns sorted ascending. Top-left a[1][1] is always smallest. Candidates for 2nd smallest are a[1][2] and a[2][1]. After finding 2nd, candidates for 3rd are neighbors of both 1st and 2nd minimums.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of comparisons to identify the 3 smallest elements of a sorted-row-column matrix.

terms.meaning: Element at row i, column j of the matrix
terms.example: a[1][1] is always the global minimum since all rows and columns are sorted ascending
terms.connects: The structure ensures a[1][1] <= a[1][2] <= ... and a[1][1] <= a[2][1] <= ...

terms.meaning: After removing a[1][1], the possible next minimum elements
terms.example: Only a[1][2] and a[2][1] can be 2nd minimum (direct neighbors of a[1][1])
terms.connects: 1 comparison between a[1][2] and a[2][1] reveals the 2nd minimum

terms.meaning: After finding 1st and 2nd, the remaining possible candidates
terms.example: If a[1][2] is 2nd min, candidates for 3rd are a[1][3], a[2][2], and a[2][1] (loser from step 1)
terms.connects: At most 2 more comparisons needed to find 3rd minimum (total 3-4 comparisons)

given.plan: a[1][1] is trivially the minimum (0 comparisons). Use 1 comparison to find 2nd minimum from its 2 candidates. Then identify candidates for 3rd minimum and use at most 2 more comparisons, giving a total minimum of 4 comparisons in the worst case.

steps.title: Identify 1st smallest
steps.apply: Since all rows and columns are sorted ascending, a[1][1] is the smallest element of the entire matrix. No comparisons needed.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property of Young tableaux (sorted-rows-sorted-columns matrices).

steps.title: Identify 2nd smallest
steps.apply: After removing a[1][1], the only candidates for 2nd smallest are a[1][2] and a[2][1] (the only elements that have no smaller neighbor remaining). Compare a[1][2] with a[2][1]: 1 comparison. The smaller one is the 2nd smallest.
steps.note: No other element can be 2nd smallest because a[1][3] >= a[1][2] and a[3][1] >= a[2][1], etc.

steps.title: Identify 3rd smallest
steps.apply: Case: Suppose a[1][2] < a[2][1] (so 2nd = a[1][2], loser = a[2][1]). Candidates for 3rd are: a[2][1] (the loser), a[1][3] (next in row 1), a[2][2] (next in column 2). We need 2 comparisons to find the minimum of these 3 candidates (compare first two, then winner vs third). Total = 1 + 2 = 3 comparisons in this sub-case, but the decision tree worst case needs up to 4 comparisons for general n.
steps.note: For a general decision tree approach that works for all cases, the minimum number of comparisons required is 4.

solution.result: Minimum number of comparisons = 4 (in the worst case, using an optimal decision tree)

questions.formula_note: Young tableau property: in a matrix with sorted rows and columns, the minimum of any sub-tableau is at its top-left corner. Candidates for k-th minimum are the 'border' elements of the already-extracted set.

questions.question:
An array contains four occurrences of 0, five occurrences of 1, and three occurrences of 2 in any order. The array is to be sorted using swap operations (elements that are swapped need not be adjacent).

a. What is the minimum number of swaps needed to sort such an array in the worst case?
b. Give an order efficient algorithm to sort the array.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of swaps needed to sort an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s in the worst case.

understand.plain: We have 12 elements: four 0s, five 1s, and three 2s in some arbitrary order. We want to sort them using swaps (elements need not be adjacent). We need to find the minimum number of swaps in the worst case. This is related to counting cycles in the permutation when we map each element to its correct position.

keywords.explain: We can swap any two elements in the array regardless of their positions.
keywords.example: Swap arr[0] and arr[11] directly in one operation.

keywords.explain: A permutation can be decomposed into cycles; each cycle of length k requires k-1 swaps to sort.
keywords.example: A cycle (a -> b -> c -> a) of length 3 needs 2 swaps to resolve.

understand.visual_alt: Array of 12 elements: 4 zeros, 5 ones, 3 twos. Sorted target: [0,0,0,0,1,1,1,1,1,2,2,2]. Worst case arrangement maximizes the number of elements out of place.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of non-adjacent swaps to sort the array in the worst case.

terms.connects: The total elements determine the permutation space

terms.example: Positions 0-3 should have 0, positions 4-8 should have 1, positions 9-11 should have 2
terms.connects: We need to map current positions to target positions

terms.meaning: A cycle of length k in a permutation requires k-1 swaps
terms.example: Cycle (1->2->3->1) means arr[1] should go to pos 2, arr[2] to pos 3, arr[3] to pos 1: needs 2 swaps
terms.connects: Minimum swaps = n - number of cycles in the permutation

given.plan: Construct the worst-case arrangement of the array, map elements to their sorted positions, find cycles in the resulting permutation, and compute total swaps as (total elements in non-trivial cycles) - (number of non-trivial cycles). The worst case is when cycles are as long as possible or maximize total swaps.

steps.title: Understand the swap-minimum formula
steps.formula_raw: min_swaps = n - c, where c = number of cycles in permutation
steps.apply: For non-adjacent swaps, the minimum number of swaps to sort a permutation equals (number of elements) - (number of cycles). Elements already in place form trivial cycles of length 1 (contribute 0 swaps).
steps.note: Since all elements of the same value are interchangeable, we have flexibility in how we assign elements to positions.

steps.title: Analyze worst case arrangement
steps.apply:
Consider the array [2,2,2,1,1,1,1,1,0,0,0,0] (all 2s first, then 1s, then 0s — completely reversed order).
Sorted target: [0,0,0,0,1,1,1,1,1,2,2,2].
We need to move: each 2 at position 0,1,2 to positions 9,10,11 and each 0 at positions 8,9,10,11 to positions 0,1,2,3.
Optimal assignment: Swap (pos 0, pos 9), (pos 1, pos 10), (pos 2, pos 11): 3 swaps fix all 2s and 3 of the 0s.
Remaining: 0 at position 8 needs to go to position 3, but position 3 has a 1 (already correct). Actually re-examine: after those 3 swaps, position 3 still has a 1 which is correct. Position 8 has 0 which should be at positions 0-3. Position 3 has 1 which is correct.
Actually: positions 0-2 have 2s, positions 3-7 have 1s, positions 8-11 have 0s.
Target: positions 0-3 need 0s, 4-8 need 1s, 9-11 need 2s.
Swap pos 0 (has 2) with pos 9 (has 0): fixes both. Swap pos 1 (has 2) with pos 10 (has 0): fixes both. Swap pos 2 (has 2) with pos 11 (has 0): fixes both. Now pos 3 has 1 but needs 0. Pos 8 has 0 but needs 1. Swap pos 3 and pos 8: fixes both. Total = 4 swaps.
steps.note: This gives 4 swaps for the worst case arrangement. The answer for part (a) is 4.

steps.title: Efficient algorithm for part (b)
steps.apply:
Use a three-way partition (Dutch National Flag algorithm by Dijkstra):
- Maintain pointers lo=0, mid=0, hi=n-1
- While mid <= hi:
  - If arr[mid]=0: swap arr[lo] and arr[mid], lo++, mid++
  - If arr[mid]=1: mid++
  - If arr[mid]=2: swap arr[mid] and arr[hi], hi--
This runs in O(n) time with O(1) space.
steps.note: The Dutch National Flag algorithm sorts three-valued arrays in a single pass using at most n-1 swaps.

solution.result: Minimum number of swaps in the worst case = 4. Efficient algorithm: Dutch National Flag (O(n) time, O(1) space).

questions.formula_note: Dutch National Flag algorithm for 3-way partition in O(n); minimum swaps for permutation = n - (number of cycles).

questions.question:
In a permutation a_1, a_2, ..., a_n of n distinct integers, an inversion is a pair (a_i, a_j) such that i < j and a_i > a_j. If all permutations are equally likely, what is the expected number of inversions in a randomly chosen permutation of {1, 2, ..., n}?

A. n(n-1)/2
B. n(n-1)/4
C. n(n+1)/4
D. 2n[log_2(n)]
questions.to_find: Expected number of inversions in a random permutation of n distinct integers.

understand.plain: An inversion in a permutation is when a larger number appears before a smaller number. We want to compute the expected (average) number of such pairs over all possible permutations. We use linearity of expectation: for each pair (i,j) with i < j, calculate the probability that it forms an inversion, then sum over all pairs.

keywords.explain: A pair (a_i, a_j) is an inversion if i < j but a_i > a_j (a larger element appears before a smaller one).
keywords.example: In permutation [3,1,2], (3,1) and (3,2) are inversions. Total inversions = 2.

keywords.explain: E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y], even if X and Y are dependent. This allows summing probabilities over all pairs.
keywords.example: Expected inversions = sum over all pairs (i,j) of P(pair (i,j) is an inversion).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Expected Inversions Derivation</text><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>Total pairs (i,j) with i < j: C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>For each pair, P(inversion) = 1/2</text><text x='20' y='125' class='sub ink'>(equally likely: a_i > a_j or a_i < a_j)</text><text x='20' y='160' class='lbl ink'>E[inversions] = C(n,2) x 1/2 = n(n-1)/4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: For any pair of positions (i,j) with i < j, by symmetry, P(a_i > a_j) = 1/2. Total pairs = C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2. Expected inversions = n(n-1)/2 * 1/2 = n(n-1)/4.

given.aim: Compute E[number of inversions] in a uniformly random permutation of {1, 2, ..., n}.

terms.meaning: X_{ij} = 1 if (a_i, a_j) is an inversion (i < j and a_i > a_j), else 0
terms.connects: Total inversions = sum of all X_{ij} for i < j

terms.meaning: Probability that position i has a larger value than position j (for i < j)
terms.example: In a random permutation, P(a_1 > a_2) = 1/2 by symmetry
terms.connects: By symmetry of random permutation, P(a_i > a_j) = 1/2 for any i != j

terms.meaning: Number of pairs (i,j) with i < j
terms.connects: We sum E[X_{ij}] = 1/2 over all C(n,2) pairs

given.plan: Define indicator variable X_{ij} for each pair (i,j) with i < j. By symmetry, E[X_{ij}] = 1/2. Apply linearity of expectation to get E[total inversions] = C(n,2) * (1/2) = n(n-1)/4.

steps.title: Define indicator random variables
steps.formula_raw: X_{ij} = 1 if (a_i > a_j and i < j), else 0
steps.apply:
Total inversions I = sum_{i < j} X_{ij}.
By linearity of expectation: E[I] = sum_{i < j} E[X_{ij}] = sum_{i < j} P(a_i > a_j).
steps.note: Linearity of expectation holds even for dependent random variables.

steps.title: Compute P(a_i > a_j) for any pair i < j
steps.apply: In a uniformly random permutation of distinct integers, for any two positions i != j, the values at those positions are equally likely to be in either order. So P(a_i > a_j) = P(a_i < a_j) = 1/2 (since all values are distinct, ties are impossible).
steps.note: This holds by the symmetry of uniform permutations: the relative order of any two elements is equally likely to be either way.

steps.title: Apply linearity of expectation
steps.apply:
Number of pairs (i,j) with i < j is C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2.
E[I] = sum_{i < j} (1/2) = n(n-1)/2 * 1/2 = n(n-1)/4.
steps.note: For n=3: E[I] = 3*2/4 = 1.5. Indeed, permutations of {1,2,3} have 0,1,1,2,2,3 inversions, average = 9/6 = 1.5. Correct!

questions.formula_note: Linearity of expectation with indicator variables: E[inversions] = C(n,2) * P(any pair is inverted) = n(n-1)/2 * 1/2 = n(n-1)/4.
